english: 1. a bit, a small piece of anything, k'am-c'uṅ (kham chung) a small bit, k'am-gáṅ (kham gang), k'am ycig (kham gcig) a mouthful, k'am-tśád-du ycód-pa (kham tshad du gcod pa) to cut 'in the size of bits' Dzl. (infernal punishment); k'am-zán (kham zan) a mouthful of food Mil.; zas k'am ycig (zas kham gcig) id. — 3. W., C. appetite, *zá-ce-la k'am yoṅ* (za ce la kham yong) W. I get an appetite for eating; *k'am dig soṅ* (kham dig song) W. I have no appetite; k'am-lóg (kham log) want of appetite, nausea, aversion (Cs. also: hatred); *k'am-lóg-pa* (kham log pa) inclined to nausea, easily sickened C.; *k'am-lóg-k'aṅ* (kham log khang) W. id.; *k'am-k'ám co dug* (kham kham co dug), *ñiṅ k'am-k'ám co dug* (nying kham kham co dug) (with la) W. he has a desire, a longing for, perh. only provincial pronunciation for rkám-pa (rkam pa).
1. fox-coloured, sorrel, brownish. — 2. porcelain-clay, china-clay. — 3. Tanacetum tomentosum, a very aromatic plant, frequent on high mountains.
a cup made of dough, used as a lamp in sacrificing.
(perh the Ar.-Hd. خمیر leaven) thick bread-cakes leavened with butter-milk Ld.

1. apricot B., C., k'ám-bui tsi-gu (kham bui tsi gu) the stone of an apricot; k'am-bu-már (kham bu mar) the oil pressed out of apricot-stones, smelling and tasting of bitter almonds Med.; mṅa-rís k'ám-bu (mnga' ris kham bu) dried apricots, v. p'á-tiṅ (pha ting). — 2. peach Sik. — 3. v. k'am (kham).
Sch. cherries, morels; these not being known in Tibet, the word must be either of Khotan or Chinese origin, or else the signification of 'stones of apricots' is to be adopted, as given in Wts.

1. physical constitution of the body, state of health, k'ams bdé ba (khams bde ba) healthy constitution, good health; rje-btsún-gyi k'ams bde lágs-sam? (rje btsun gyi khams bde lags sam?) is your Reverence well? asks a layman, and the Lama answers: ṅa śín-tu bde; k'yed k'ams bde-am? (nga shin tu bde; khyed khams bde am?) I am quite well; are you well? Mil.; W. more frq.: *k'am-záṅ-po* (kham zang po), C. also k'am sáṅ (kham sang) good health; k'ams-rmyá (khams rmya) Med., acc. to Cs. nausea, feeling sick; k'ams-sós (khams sos) Sch.: rest, comfort, health, prob. more accurately: recreation, recovery, restoration (of health), so: k'ams sós-par gyur (khams sos par gyur) Mil.; sometimes it seems to be a synonym of lus (lus), body, k'ams dúb-pa bsó-ba (khams dub pa bso ba) to recreate the exhausted body Mil.nt. fig.: gnod-sems-méd-pai k'ams śas c'e (gnod sems med pai khams shas che) the peaceable disposition predominates Stg. — 2. (synon. of yul (yul)) empire, realm, territory, domain; yul-k'áms (yul khams) empire, in a geographical and political sense, e.g. Nepaul Glr.; rgyal-k'áms (rgyal khams) 1. for rgyál-poi k'ams (rgyal poi khams) kingdom, Tibet. 2. for rgyál-bai k'ams (rgyal bai khams) the empire of Buddha, the world; rgyal-k'áms 'grím-pa (rgyal khams 'grim pa) to roam over the kingdoms, the countries Mil.; region, dominion, bar-snáṅ-gyi k'ams (bar snang gyi khams) the aërial regions, where the lha (lha) live Pth.; in physiology: mk'rís-pai k'ams (mkhris pai khams) the dominion of bile Med.; k'ams gsum (khams gsum) the three worlds acc. to Buddhistic speculation, viz. the earth with the six heavens of the gods, as the 'region of desire', 'dód-pai k'ams ('dod pai khams); above this is the 'region of form', gzúgs-kyi k'ams (gzugs kyi khams), and ultimately follows the 'region of formlessness', gzugs-méd-pai k'ams (gzugs med pai khams). — 3. element (syn. 'byúṅ-ba ('byung ba)), k'ams drug (khams drug) the six elements of some philosophical systems, consisting, besides the four elements familiar to us, also of nám-mk'a (nam mkha') and rnam-śés (rnam shes), the ether and the substance of the mind. In chronology, in naming the single years of the cycle, five elements are assumed, which (according to Chinese theory) are wood, fire, earth, iron, water. — 4. p.n. Khams, Great Tibet, the parts between Ü and China; smad-mdo-k'ams-sgóṅ gsum (smad mdo khams sgong gsum) the low-land, the three provinces Do, Kham, and Gong, cf. mṅa-rís (mnga' ris); k'áms-pa (khams pa) a man from Khams.
steatite, soapstone, Sch.. prob. = dkar-goṅ (dkar gong).
